characters.name: Carruthers
characters.description: A young man of condition and fashion who works at the Foreign Office, stuck in London during the dead season of September, feeling isolated and depressed about his social situation.

physical.appearance: well-groomed, not very tall, fashionable attire

characters.name: Davies
characters.description: A man who accompanied Carruthers on the yachting cruise and was involved in discovering information related to national security.

characters.name: E. C.
characters.description: The editor of the book who received the story from Carruthers and arranged for its publication.

characters.name: Arthur H. Davies
characters.description: Carruthers' former Oxford college acquaintance who owns a yacht called Dulcibella and is currently in Flensburg. He failed for the Indian Civil Service and works in a solicitor's office. Known for his physical energy, simplicity, and modesty, with a connection to boats and the sea.

physical.appearance: dresses indifferently, short stature, blue eyes, clean-cut features, unintellectual forehead, horny hands stained with paint, bandaged hand, informal clothing

characters.name: Lady Ashleigh
characters.description: A society hostess who wrote kindly to Carruthers about missing him at the Morven Lodge party.

characters.name: Nesta
characters.description: Carruthers' cousin who wrote to him about his work in London, described as malicious.

characters.name: Withers
characters.description: A servant who delivers mail to Carruthers in his chambers.

characters.name: Carter
characters.description: Carruthers' subordinate at the Foreign Office who assists with various tasks including map work and sending telegrams.

characters.name: K——
characters.description: Carruthers' colleague who returns from Switzerland, sunburnt and envious of Carruthers' planned trip.

characters.name: M——
characters.description: Carruthers' temporary chief at the Foreign Office who wishes him well on his holiday.

characters.name: Davies sister
characters.description: Davies' sister whose photograph hangs in the yacht's cabin.

characters.name: Hebrew merchant
characters.description: A dirty and jewelled merchant in the east end who haggles with Carruthers over oilskins.

timeline.event: Carruthers and Davies go on a yachting cruise and discover important national security information
The discovered information is communicated to proper authorities
Carruthers visits E. C. in his chambers and tells him the whole adventure
Decision is made to publish the story
E. C. agrees to edit the book
Carruthers spends the dead season alone in London, working at the Foreign Office
Arthur H. Davies starts his yachting cruise via Holland and the Frisian Islands
Davies' companions leave him during the cruise
Davies writes to Carruthers from Flensburg inviting him to join the yachting trip
Carruthers receives Davies' letter while dressing for dinner
Carruthers decides to accept Davies' invitation after breakfast
K—— returns from Switzerland to the Foreign Office
Carruthers sends telegram to Davies accepting invitation
Davies replies requesting Rippingille stove
Carruthers spends evening shopping for Davies' commissions
Carruthers continues shopping for nautical supplies in the Minories
Carruthers finishes work at the Foreign Office and says goodbye to M——
Carruthers departs London for Germany on the Flushing steamer
Carruthers travels from Flushing to Hamburg to Flensburg
Carruthers meets Davies at Flensburg station
Davies and Carruthers travel by dinghy to the Dulcibella
Carruthers boards the Dulcibella and settles in for the night

settings.name: E. C.'s chambers
settings.description: The location where Carruthers visited E. C. to tell him about the adventure

settings.name: German waters
settings.description: The general location where the yachting cruise and discovery of secret information took place, involving the German Government

settings.name: Carruthers' chambers in Pall Mall
settings.description: Carruthers' London residence where he dresses for dinner and receives correspondence

settings.name: Foreign Office
settings.description: Where Carruthers works, doing routine tasks like making précis of consular reports during the dead season

settings.name: London club
settings.description: A substitute club Carruthers visits for dinner, described as strange and uncomfortable with poor food and ventilation

settings.name: Flensburg, Schleswig-Holstein
settings.description: German port town where Davies is stationed with his yacht Dulcibella, having repairs done

settings.name: Schleswig fiords
settings.description: Baltic cruising ground described by Davies as having A1 scenery and potential for duck shooting

settings.name: Yacht Dulcibella
settings.description: Davies' yacht, currently moored in Flensburg for repairs

settings.name: Lancaster's gun shop
settings.description: Shop where Carruthers retrieves Davies' gun and pays an outstanding bill

settings.name: The Stores
settings.description: Large department store where Carruthers purchases the Rippingille stove and inquires about rigging-screws

settings.name: The Minories
settings.description: East London area where Carey and Neilson's shop is located, specializing in rigging-screws and nautical equipment

settings.name: Carey and Neilson's
settings.description: Nautical equipment shop in the Minories that stocks rigging-screws

settings.name: East end oilskin shop
settings.description: A villainous den in a back street where Carruthers purchases cheap oilskins from a Hebrew merchant

settings.name: Flushing steamer
settings.description: Passenger steamship that carries Carruthers from London to Hamburg via Flushing

settings.name: Thames estuary
settings.description: Waterway leading from London to the North Sea, lined with lightships

settings.name: North Sea
settings.description: Body of water crossed by the steamer from England to Holland/Germany

settings.name: Hamburg
settings.description: German port city, intermediate stop on Carruthers' journey to Flensburg

settings.name: Flensburg railway station
settings.description: Station where Carruthers arrives and meets Davies

settings.name: Flensburg harbour
settings.description: Harbour area with quays, steamers, and stairs leading down to the water where small boats moor

settings.name: Small cove near Flensburg
settings.description: Tree-encircled cove where the Dulcibella is anchored, away from the main town

settings.name: Dulcibella interior
settings.description: Cramped yacht interior with saloon, sleeping cabin, low headroom, centreboard case, galley with Rippingille stove, and basic furnishings

world_rules: Information discovered about national security must be communicated to proper authorities
Government secret service may be inadequate and show dignified incredulity toward citizen reports
Mere suspicion of certain secrets can destroy their efficacy
Personal identity protection requires fictitious names and disguised years when publishing sensitive accounts
London society empties during the dead season of late summer/early autumn
Foreign Office work involves routine administrative tasks during quiet periods
Social connections and proper introductions are essential for maintaining position in society
Yachting season typically ends by late September as weather becomes dangerous
Small yachts like the Dulcibella are typically sailed single-handed without paid crew
Flat-bottomed boats with centreboards can navigate both shallow and deep waters
Nautical equipment must often be sourced from specialized suppliers in port areas
Continental travel requires careful planning with railway timetables and connections
Yacht accommodations are spartan compared to larger vessels with professional crew

voice_profile.style_notes: Editorial framing device, diplomatic language to protect identities, formal Victorian prose style

plot_threads.thread: What was the nature of the national danger discovered during the cruise?
What information was discovered about the German Government?
Who is the disgracefully implicated Englishman bearing an honoured name?
Will the government act on the information provided by Carruthers and Davies?
Will Carruthers accept Davies' invitation to join him yachting in the Baltic?
Why did Davies' companions leave him during the cruise?
What is the significance of Davies requesting a prismatic compass?
How did Davies injure his hand and why is he secretive about it?
What mysterious activities has Davies been engaged in that required so much secrecy?
Why does Davies need surveying equipment like the prismatic compass if this is just a pleasure cruise?
Will Carruthers adapt to the harsh realities of small boat sailing?
